name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
قُل لِّمَن مَّا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ قُل لِّلَّهِ ۚ كَتَبَ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِ ٱلرَّحْمَةَ ۚ لَيَجْمَعَنَّكُمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ ۚ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ فَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٢)
```

```english
Say, ‘To whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth?’ Say, ‘To Allah. He has made mercy incumbent upon Himself. He will surely gather you on the Day of Resurrection, in which there is no doubt. Those who have ruined their souls will not have faith.’ (12)
```

```arabic
۞ وَلَهُۥ مَا سَكَنَ فِى ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (١٣)
```

```english
To Him belongs whatever abides in the night and the day, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (13)
```

```arabic
قُلْ أَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ أَتَّخِذُ وَلِيًّا فَاطِرِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَهُوَ يُطْعِمُ وَلَا يُطْعَمُ ۗ قُلْ إِنِّىٓ أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ أَوَّلَ مَنْ أَسْلَمَ ۖ وَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (١٤)
```

```english
Say, ‘Shall I take for guardian [anyone] other than Allah, the originator of the heavens and the earth, who feeds and is not fed?’ Say, ‘I have been commanded to be the first of those who submit [to Allah], and [told,] “Never be one of the polytheists.” ’ (14)
```

```arabic
قُلْ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّى عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٥)
```

```english
Say, ‘Indeed, should I disobey my Lord, I fear the punishment of a tremendous day.’ (15)
```

```arabic
مَّن يُصْرَفْ عَنْهُ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَقَدْ رَحِمَهُۥ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (١٦)
```

```english
Whoever is spared of it on that day, He has certainly been merciful to him, and that is manifest success. (16)
```

```arabic
وَإِن يَمْسَسْكَ ٱللَّهُ بِضُرٍّ فَلَا كَاشِفَ لَهُۥٓ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ وَإِن يَمْسَسْكَ بِخَيْرٍ فَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٧)
```

```english
Should Allah visit you with some distress there is no one to remove it except Him; and should He bring you some good, then He has power over all things. (17)
```

```arabic
وَهُوَ ٱلْقَاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبَادِهِۦ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَكِيمُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ (١٨)
```

```english
And He is the All-dominant over His servants, and He is the All-wise, the All-aware. (18)
```

## (Commentary)

A group of verses argues against the polytheists (المشركين) regarding monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and the Return (المعاد) — *the restoration of life after death for judgment*. The first two verses contain the proof for the Return, while the remaining five verses are directed toward monotheism, establishing the proof for it from two perspectives, as will follow.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ لِمَنْ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ قُلْ لِلَّهِ** — "Say, ‘To whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth?’ Say, ‘To Allah.’" (6:12). This is the commencement of the demonstration for the Return. Its summary is that Allah, the Exalted, is the Owner (مالك) of all that is in the heavens and the earth; He has the right to dispose of them however He wills and desires. He, the Glorified, has characterized Himself with the attribute of mercy (الرحمة) — *the lifting of the need of every needy being and the bringing of everything to what it deserves, bestowing it upon them*. A number of His servants, including human beings, are fit for eternal life and prepared to be felicitous therein. Therefore, by the requirement of His ownership and His mercy, He will certainly dispose of them by gathering them and giving them what they deserve.

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ لِمَنْ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** (Say, ‘To whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth?’) etc., contains one of the premises of the argument. His saying: **كَتَبَ عَلى نَفْسِهِ الرَّحْمَةَ** (He has prescribed mercy for Himself) contains another premise. And His saying: **وَلَهُ ما سَكَنَ فِي اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ** (To Him belongs whatever dwells in the night and the day) (6:13) etc., is a third premise serving as a part of the argument.

So, His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ لِمَنْ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** etc., commands the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to ask them about who owns the heavens and the earth and has the right to dispose of them as He wills without any preventer hindering Him. This is Allah, the Glorified, without doubt, because anything other than Him—even the idols and the lords of the idols whom the polytheists invoke—are like the rest of creation; their creation and their command terminate in Him, the Exalted. Thus, He is the Owner of all that is in the heavens and the earth.

Because the subject of the question is known and clear to both the asker and the asked, and the opponent (الخصم) acknowledges it, there was no need for the answer to be issued by the opponent or for his verbal admission. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was commanded to state the answer himself and take charge of it so that the proof would be completed without waiting for their response.

Asking the opponent and the questioner himself undertaking the answer are both among the exquisite stylistic devices (السلائق البديعة) used in the presentation of arguments. A benefactor says to the one he has bestowed favors upon, who then showed ingratitude for his blessing: "Who is it that fed you, gave you drink, and clothed you? It is I who did that for you and favored you with it, yet you repay me with ingratitude."

In short, it is established by this question and answer that Allah, the Glorified, is the Absolute Owner (المالك على الإطلاق). He has the right to dispose of things as He wills—of giving life, providing sustenance, causing death, and resurrecting after death—without any preventer hindering Him, such as precision in work, death, absence, disorder, or otherwise. With this, one of the premises of the argument is completed, so He joined it with the other premise, which is His saying: **كَتَبَ عَلى نَفْسِهِ الرَّحْمَةَ** (He has prescribed mercy for Himself).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَتَبَ عَلى نَفْسِهِ الرَّحْمَةَ** — "He has prescribed mercy for Himself." Writing (الكتابة) is the act of establishing and the final decree (القضاء الحتم). Since mercy (الرحمة) — *the bestowing of grace upon its deserving recipient and bringing a thing to the felicity that befits it* — is among His active attributes (صفاته الفعلية), it is correct to attribute it to His writing. The meaning is: He has made mercy, the bestowing of blessings, and the sending down of good mandatory upon Himself for those who deserve it.

Similar to this in attributing an act to "writing" and the like is His saying, the Exalted: **كَتَبَ اللهُ لَأَغْلِبَنَّ أَنَا وَرُسُلِي** — "Allah has ordained: ‘I shall surely prevail, I and My apostles.’" (58:21), and His saying: **فَوَ رَبِّ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ** — "By the Lord of the sky and the earth, it is indeed the truth" (51:23). As for the attributes of the essence (صفات الذات) such as life (الحياة), knowledge (العلم), and power (القدرة), it is not correct to attribute them to "writing" and the like at all; it is not said: "He wrote life, knowledge, and power upon Himself."

The necessary consequence of His prescribing mercy for Himself—as previously mentioned—is that He completes His blessing upon them by gathering them for the Day of Resurrection to recompense them for their words and deeds, whereby the believers (المؤمنون) succeed and others lose.

Therefore, He concluded with His saying, which is like the conclusion (النتيجة) of the argument: **لَيَجْمَعَنَّكُمْ إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ** — "He will surely gather you on the Day of Resurrection, in which there is no doubt." (6:12). He emphasized the meaning with the most eloquent emphasis: the *lam* of the oath, the *nun* of emphasis, and His saying: **لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ** (in which there is no doubt).

Then He pointed out that the profit on this day is for the believers and the loss is upon others, saying: **الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ فَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ** — "Those who have ruined their souls will not have faith." (6:12).

The argument established in this verse for the Return is different from the two arguments established for it in His saying, the Exalted: **وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماءَ وَالْأَرْضِ وَما بَيْنَهُما باطِلاً ذلِكَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَوَيْلٌ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنَ النَّارِ ، أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ** — "We did not create the sky and the earth and whatever is between them in vain. That is the opinion of the faithless; so woe to the faithless from the Fire! Shall We treat those who have faith and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption on the earth? Shall We treat the God-conscious like the vicious?" (38:27-28). Those two verses establish the proof for the Return from the perspective that His act, the Exalted, is not in vain (باطل) but has a purpose (غاية), and from the perspective that equating the believer with the disbeliever and the God-conscious (المتقي) with the vicious (الفاجر) is an injustice (ظلم) that does not befit Him, the Exalted. Since they are not distinguished in this world, there must be another realm (نشأة أخرى) in which they are distinguished by felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاوة). This is different from what is in this verse, which proceeds toward the objective through the path of mercy.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَهُ ما سَكَنَ فِي اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ وَهُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ** — "To Him belongs whatever dwells in the night and the day, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing." (6:13). Dwelling (السكون) in the night and the day is to occur within the vessel of this natural world (العالم الطبيعي), whose affairs are managed by night and day. Its system (نظام) runs by the covering of the pouring light from a luminous sun, and the operation of light transformations within it through proximity and distance, abundance and scarcity, presence and absence, alignment, and other factors.

The night and the day are the general cradle in which the universal elements (العناصر الكلية) and their offspring are nurtured—a nurturing that drives every part of its parts and every individual of its individuals toward the goal (غاية) destined for it, completing it in spirit and body.

Just as the general and specific dwelling place (المسكن) has a complete influence on the being of the dweller (الساكن)—for example, a human being dwells on a land, roaming it in search of sustenance, being nourished by the grains and fruits it produces and the animals raised upon it, drinking its water, breathing its air, and being affected by the qualities of its region, with all parts of his body growing according to its measure—likewise, the night and the day have a complete influence on the formation of everything that comes into being within them.

The human being is among the things dwelling in the night and the day. He came into being by the will of Allah from the combination of simple and compound parts into a specific form. His existence is distinguished in its inception and survival by a life based on intellectual perception (شعور فكري) and will (إرادة), which are prepared for him by internal emotional powers that command him to attract benefits and repel harms. These powers call him to establish a structured society (مجتمع) in which we see the details of communication through languages, the agreement to follow norms, laws, and customs in social interactions and transactions, and the respect for general opinions and beliefs regarding beauty and ugliness (الحسن والقبح), justice and injustice (العدل والظلم), obedience and disobedience (الطاعة والمعصية), and reward, punishment, recompense, and pardon.

Since Allah, the Glorified, is the Creator of the night and the day and what dwells within them, being unique in bringing them into existence, then to Him belongs whatever dwells in the night and the day. He is the True Owner (المالك الحق) of all the night and the day, their inhabitants, and the events, actions, and words that follow their existence. To Him belongs the system running within them in its wondrous vastness. Thus, He is the All-hearing (السميع) of our words, including sounds and signs, and the All-knowing (العليم) of our deeds and actions with their attributes of beauty and ugliness, justice and injustice, benevolence and wrongdoing, and what the souls acquire of felicity and wretchedness.

How could ignorance of that be possible for Him when everything has originated in His kingdom and by His permission? The mode of existence of this type of matter—I mean beauty and ugliness, justice and injustice, obedience and disobedience, as well as the languages indicating mental meanings—all of these are cognitive matters (أمور علمية) that have no reality except within the vessel of knowledge (ظرف العلم). Therefore, we see that an act does not occur from us as beautiful or ugly, nor as obedience or disobedience, and a composed sound is not called "speech" unless we know it and intend its purpose.

How is it possible for something that is cognitive (علمي) in itself, by virtue of being so, to be owned, and then its owner be ignorant of it and not know it? (Reflect deeply on this).

Allah, the Glorified, is the One who brought this world into existence in its wondrous vastness—in its simple, elemental, and compound parts—according to a system that dazzles the intellect. Then He created us and settled us in the night and the day, then multiplied us and ran among us the system of human society. Then He guided us to the formulation of languages, the consideration of norms, and the establishment of conventional concepts (الاعتبارات). He has never ceased to accompany us and all other causes (الأسباب) step by step, running with us and them in the course of night and day moment by moment, driving innumerable events, one after another.

When one of us speaks a word, He places the meaning in his heart by His inspiration (إلهام), runs the word on his tongue by His making it known, makes the sound heard by the listener by His making him hear, carries its meaning to his mind by His preservation, and makes the meaning understood by his thinking faculty by His teaching. Then He motivates him to agree with what the speaker conveyed to him or prevents him from that by a will that drives him toward it or a dislike that repels him from it. In all these stages, which the fingers of counting and enumeration fail to encompass, He is the Leader, the Driver, the Guide, the Preserver, and the Watcher (الرقيب)! How can a speaker say anything except that He, the Exalted, is All-hearing, All-knowing? "There is no secret talk between three but He is their fourth, nor between five but He is their sixth, nor less than that nor more but He is with them wherever they may be. Then He will inform them of what they did on the Day of Resurrection. Indeed Allah has knowledge of all things." (58:7).

Likewise, when one of us performs a deed, whether good or evil, it is born of a father and a mother who give birth to it under the supervision of choice (الاختيار) and will (الإرادة). It has transitioned to them after traversing a long path and a lengthy period in the loins of its active causes and the wombs of other passive causes, until it terminates in that which Allah knows best. He, the Glorified, has never ceased to move it by His will from one lap to another—the earth is His handful and the heavens are in His right hand—until it descended into the station of choice. He accompanied it station after station by His permission until it rose from the horizon of external reality (العين) and took its place in the dwelling of night and day. Then it continues to run through the parts of the cosmos that are affected by it as one of the running causes, while Allah, the Glorified, is a Witness (شهيد) over it and Encompassing (محيط) it. How is it possible for Him, the Glorified, to be heedless of that which is of this nature? "Does He who created not know, while He is the All-attentive, the All-aware?" (67:14).

From what has preceded, it becomes clear that His saying, the Exalted: **وَهُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ** (and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing) is in the position of a conclusion (النتيجة) for His saying: **وَلَهُ ما سَكَنَ فِي اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ** (To Him belongs whatever dwells in the night and the day).

Hearing (السمع) and knowledge (العلم), although counted among His essential attributes (صفاته الذاتية) which are identical to the Transcendent Essence (عين الذات) without being branched from anything else, there is a type of knowledge—as well as hearing and sight—that is an active attribute (صفة فعلية) external to the Essence. Its establishment depends on the realization of an object (متعلق) other than the Holy Essence, such as creating (الخلق), providing (الرزق), giving life (الإحياء), and causing death (الإماتة), which depend on the existence of a creature, a recipient of sustenance, a living being, and a dead being.

Since things in their very selves and essences are owned and encompassed by Him, the Exalted, then if they are sounds, they are "heard" and "audible" to Him, the Exalted; if they are lights and colors, they are "seen" and "visible" to Him, the Exalted; and all of them, whatever they may be, are "knowledge" and "known" to Him, the Exalted. This type of knowledge is among His active attributes that are realized upon the realization of the act from Him, the Exalted, not before that. Their establishment after they were not does not necessitate change in His Essence, the Exalted and Sanctified, because they do not exceed the station of the act (مقام الفعل) and do not enter the realm of the Essence (عالم الذات). Thus, the verse, in its derivation of knowledge from ownership, intends to establish active knowledge (العلم الفعلي); so understand that.

The verse—I mean His saying: **وَلَهُ ما سَكَنَ فِي اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ** etc.—is like one of the premises of the argument explained in the previous verse. For although the argument for the Return was completed by His saying: **قُلْ لِمَنْ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ قُلْ لِلَّهِ كَتَبَ عَلى نَفْسِهِ الرَّحْمَةَ**, a simple, initial glance might overlook the fact that His ownership of things necessitates His knowledge of them and His hearing of what is heard from them, such as sounds and words.

Therefore, He drew attention to it by repeating the ownership of the heavens and the earth and branching hearing and knowledge upon it, saying: **وَلَهُ ما سَكَنَ فِي اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ** —which is in the meaning of His saying: "To Him belongs what is in the heavens and the earth"— **وَهُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ**. Thus, this verse was, for that reason, in the position of a premise completing the argument presented in the previous verse.

The verse—though we have not exhausted its right, nor will it ever be exhausted—is among the most delicate of Quranic verses in meaning, the most precise in indication and proof, and the most eloquent in logic.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ أَغَيْرَ اللهِ أَتَّخِذُ وَلِيًّا فاطِرِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَهُوَ يُطْعِمُ وَلا يُطْعَمُ** — "Say, ‘Shall I take for guardian anyone other than Allah, the Originator of the heavens and the earth, who feeds and is not fed?’" (6:14). This is the commencement of the argumentation for His uniqueness, the Exalted, and that He has no partner.

What is gathered from the history of paganism (الوثنية) and the taking of idols and deities is that they only followed that and submitted to the deities for one of two reasons: Either they found themselves in need of many causes for the preservation of life, such as nourishment through food, clothing, shelter, spouses, children, kin, and the like—the most central of which is food, for which the human need is more intense than the need for anything else according to a simple view. They believed that every category of these needs was connected to a cause (سبب) that bestowed upon them the enjoyment of the means to lift that need, such as the cause that makes the sky rain so that the pasture and fodder grow for their beasts and provides fertility for themselves, or the cause that manages the affairs of the plain and the mountain, or inspires love and intimacy, or to whom the affair of the sea and the ships running therein belongs.

Then they found that their power was insufficient to master those necessary needs, so they were forced to submit to the cause linked to their need, taking it as a god and then worshipping it.

Or because they found this defenseless human being to be a target for the arrows of events, surrounded by great general harms and evils that he cannot resist, such as floods, earthquakes, deluges, famine, and plague, and by other innumerable particular afflictions and trials, such as diseases, pains, falls, poverty, infertility, enemies, the envious, the hater, and others. Then they assigned to these things powerful causes that send these afflictions to them, breaking their backs with them and darkening the purity of their lives. These are celestial creatures such as the lords of the species (أرباب الأنواع) and the spirits of the stars and celestial bodies. They took them as gods out of fear of their wrath and punishment, and worshipped them to win them over through worship and please them through submission and humility, thereby escaping from harms and calamities and being safe from their evils and the injuries descending from them.

The verse—I mean His saying: **قُلْ أَغَيْرَ اللهِ أَتَّخِذُ وَلِيًّا** etc.—and the verses following it argue against the polytheists by turning their own two arguments back upon them. That is, it accepts the basis of the argument and considers it true, but explains that its necessary consequence is to worship Allah, the Glorified, alone, and to negate every posited partner from Him.

So His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ أَغَيْرَ اللهِ أَتَّخِذُ وَلِيًّا فاطِرِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَهُوَ يُطْعِمُ وَلا يُطْعَمُ** is an indication of the argument from the first path, which is the path of hope (الرجاء)—that the deity is worshipped because He is a Benefactor (منعم), so worshipping Him is gratitude for His favor and a cause for its increase.

He, the Glorified, commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to explain to them in the form of an inquiry and a question that Allah, the Glorified, alone is the Guardian (الولي) of the blessing that the human being and others enjoy, because He is the Provider (الرازق) who does not need anyone else to provide for Him; He feeds and is not fed. The proof for this is that He, the Exalted, is the One who originated (فطر) the heavens and the earth, bringing them out from the darkness of non-existence into the light of existence, and favored them with the blessing of realization and stability. Then He bestowed upon them blessings that none can count but He for the preservation of their existence, among which is the feeding of the human being and others. For all these blessings prepared for the preservation of the existence of the human being and others, and the causes that drive those blessings to the places of desert, all of that terminates in His origination and His bringing into existence of things, causes, and their effects altogether as His handiwork.

Thus, to Him, the Glorified, returns the provision (الرزق), of which the most important manifestation for the human being is feeding. Therefore, it is mandatory to worship Allah alone because He is the One who feeds us without needing to be fed by anyone else.

It has become clear from what we have explained: First, that the expression of servitude (العبودية) and deification (التأله) as "taking a guardian" (اتخاذ الولي) in His saying: **أَغَيْرَ اللهِ أَتَّخِذُ وَلِيًّا** is only because the argument is directed from the perspective of His favor, the Exalted, in feeding.

Second, that the reliance upon His saying: **فاطِرِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** (the Originator of the heavens and the earth) is only to explain the reason for the confinement of feeding to Him, the Exalted, as previously established. Perhaps this is also understood from the allusion (التعريض) in His saying: **وَلا يُطْعَمُ** (and is not fed), for in it is an allusion to the fact that all others whom they have taken as gods are in need—like Jesus and others—of feeding or what serves its purpose.

It is possible that it be understood from its mention in the argument that it is an indication of another path in establishing the proof for His uniqueness, the Exalted, which is nobler than both previous paths. Its summary is that Allah, the Glorified, is the Originator of this world, and to His origination (فطرة) everything terminates; therefore, submission to Him is mandatory.

The reason this path is nobler is that the other two paths, although they result in the monotheism of the deity from the perspective that He is the Worshipped, they are not free from something—namely, that they result in the obligation of worshipping Him out of greed for blessing or fear of punishment. Thus, the primary objective is the acquisition of blessing or safety from punishment rather than Him, the Exalted and Sanctified. As for this path, it results in the obligation of worshipping Allah because He is Allah, the Glorified.

Third, that the specific mention of feeding (الإطعام) from among His blessings, the Exalted, despite their abundance, is due to the consideration that feeding, according to a simple view, is the most obvious of the needs of a living animal, including the human being.

Then He, the Glorified, commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله after the completion of the argument to mention to them what supports this rational proof (الحجة العقلية)—which is that Allah commanded him through revelation (الوحي) to proceed in taking a deity upon the path to which the intellect guides, which is monotheism, and explicitly forbade him from overstepping it to join the polytheists, saying: **قُلْ إِنِّي أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ أَوَّلَ مَنْ أَسْلَمَ** — "Say, ‘I have been commanded to be the first of those who submit [to Allah],’" then He said: **وَلا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ** — "and [I was told], ‘Do not be one of the polytheists.’" (6:14).

Two matters remain here:

One: That His saying: **أَوَّلَ مَنْ أَسْلَمَ** (the first of those who submit), if the meaning is the first of those who submit from among you, then it is apparent, for he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله submitted before his nation. If the meaning is the first of those who submit without restriction, as is the apparent sense of the absolute expression, then his priority in that is according to rank (الرتبة) rather than time (الزمان).

Two: That since the result of the argument was servitude (العبودية), which is a type of humility and submission (تسليم), the use of the word "Islam" (الإسلام) in this context was more appropriate than the word "faith" (الإيمان) because of its indication of the purpose of worship, which is humility.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ إِنِّي أَخافُ إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّي عَذابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ** — "Say, ‘I fear, if I should disobey my Lord, the punishment of a tremendous day.’" (6:15). This is the second path of the two paths previously mentioned that the polytheists relied upon in taking deities—namely, that the worship of their deities secures them from the encompassment of their wrath and the descent of their punishment.

He, the Glorified, has taken in the argument the most fearful thing that must be feared among the types of punishment—and He commanded it—which is the punishment of the Hour that weighs heavily in the heavens and the earth, just as He took in the first argument the most needed thing that the human being requires according to the first glance among blessings, which is feeding.

It was said: **إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّي** (if I should disobey my Lord) rather than saying "if I should associate partners with my Lord," as an indication of what was in His saying, the Exalted, in the previous verse: **وَلا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ** (Do not be one of the polytheists), regarding the prohibition of him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله from polytheism (الشرك). Thus, the verse conveyed that it is rationally mandatory for me to worship Allah alone to be safe from what I fear of the punishment of a tremendous day, and that which the intellect indicated, the revelation from my Lord has guided me to.

By this, this verse corresponds to the previous verse from the perspective of establishing the rational proof first, then supporting it with revelation from Allah, the Glorified; so understand that. This is among the subtleties of the Quran's conciseness, for it sufficed in conveying this meaning in its vastness by merely placing His saying: **عَصَيْتُ** (I disobey) in the place of "I associate partners."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مَنْ يُصْرَفْ عَنْهُ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَقَدْ رَحِمَهُ** — "Anyone from whom it is averted on that day, He has indeed had mercy on him." (6:16) etc. The meaning is clear. The verse completes the argument presented in the previous verse. For the apparent sense of the previous verse, according to a simple view, is the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله establishing the proof for the obligation of monotheism upon himself, in that Allah forbade him from polytheism, so monotheism is mandatory upon him to be safe from the punishment of the Hereafter.

It might occur to the view of the heedless one who does not reflect to counter the argument by saying that since the prohibition was specific to you—as you claim—then the fear is specific, and then the obligation of monotheism and the negation of a partner is also specific to you. Thus, the argument would be against you and not against others.

So He conveyed by His saying: **مَنْ يُصْرَفْ عَنْهُ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَقَدْ رَحِمَهُ** that His punishment is looming over everyone and encompassing all; there is no escape from it except by His mercy. Therefore, every human being must fear for himself from the punishment of that day what the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله fears for himself. Thus, the argument is general, established against all people, not specific to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنْ يَمْسَسْكَ اللهُ بِضُرٍّ فَلا كاشِفَ لَهُ إِلَّا هُوَ** — "Should Allah visit you with any distress, there is no one to remove it except Him" (6:17) to the end of the verse. The two arguments mentioned in the previous verses had taken a sample of what the human being hopes for, which is feeding, and a sample of what he fears, which is the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, and completed the explanation with them. They did not address other types of distress (الضر) and categories of good (الخير) with which Allah, the Glorified, visits the human being, and all of it is from Allah, exalted is His Name.

So the verse clarifies by explicit statement that there is distress other than the punishment of the Day of Resurrection with which Allah, the Glorified, visits the human being, and one must turn to Him, the Exalted, for its removal. And that there is good with which Allah visits the human being, and there is none to repel His grace (فضله) nor any preventer to hinder its bestowal, due to His power over all things. The hope for good makes it mandatory for the human being to take Him, the Glorified, as a worshipped God.

Since it might be imagined that His visiting the human being with distress or good only necessitates that He be taken as a worshipped one—and the opponent does not deny that (1)—but as for the confinement of divinity (الألوهية) and worshipfulness (المعبودية) to Him, the Exalted, it does not, because what they have taken as gods are intermediate causes (أسباب متوسطة) and powerful intercessors (شفعاء أقوياء) that have effects in the cosmos of evil or good, which makes it mandatory for the human being to seek proximity to them out of fear of their evil or hope for their good.

He refuted this by stating that Allah, the Glorified, is the All-conquering (القاهر) above His servants; none of them surpasses Him nor equals Him. They themselves are under His conquest, as are their actions and effects. They perform no deed of good or evil except by His permission and His will, not being independent in any matter at all, nor possessing for themselves any harm or benefit or anything else. So whatever effect of good or evil arises from the horizon of their essences terminates in His command, His will, and His permission, being attributed to Him in a manner that befits the courtyard of His sanctity and His might.

Thus, both verses together complete a single meaning: that whatever reaches the human being of good or evil is from Allah, according to the attribution that befits His courtyard. So Allah, the Glorified, is the One unique in divinity and the One solitary in worshipfulness; there is no god but He, and no worshipped one besides Him.

He expressed the occurrence of distress and good as "touching" (المس), which indicates insignificance, in His saying: **إِنْ يَمْسَسْكَ** (should He visit/touch you) and **وَإِنْ يَمْسَسْكَ**, to indicate by it that what reaches the human being of distress or good is a small thing from what is carried by the infinite power (القدرة غير المتناهية) which nothing can withstand, nor can any limited creature endure or bear it.

It is as if His saying, the Exalted, regarding the side of good: **فَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ** (He has power over all things) was placed in the position of something like our saying: "there is no preventer to hinder Him," to indicate that He, the Exalted, is powerful over every assumed good just as He is powerful over every assumed distress. By this, the reason for His saying: **فَلا كاشِفَ لَهُ إِلَّا هُوَ** (there is no one to remove it except Him) is revealed; for if anyone other than Him, the Exalted, were to remove anything of the distress He visited, that would repel His power over it. Likewise, His power over all things necessitates that nothing can repel what He visits of good.

The specification of the distress or good visited upon the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in this verse is similar to the specification occurring in His saying: **قُلْ إِنِّي أَخافُ إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّي عَذابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ**. And His saying: **وَهُوَ الْقاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبادِهِ** (He is the All-conquering, above His servants) provides a generalization similar to what His saying: **مَنْ يُصْرَفْ عَنْهُ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَقَدْ رَحِمَهُ** provided.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَهُوَ الْقاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبادِهِ وَهُوَ الْحَكِيمُ الْخَبِيرُ** — "He is the All-conquering, above His servants, and He is the All-wise, the All-aware." (6:18). Conquest (القهر) is a type of overcoming (الغلبة); it is for a thing to prevail over another thing, forcing it to comply with an effect from the overcomer that opposes the effect the overcome has by nature or the like—such as water prevailing over fire, conquering it into extinction, and fire conquering water, evaporating it or drying its moisture. Since the cosmic causes (الأسباب الكونية) were only manifested by Allah, the Glorified, to be intermediaries in the occurrence of events, placing their effects in their results, and they—whatever they may be—are forced to comply with what Allah, the Glorified, intends in them and through them, it is true of them generally that they are conquered (مقهورة) by Allah, the Glorified; so Allah is the All-conquering (قاهر) over them.

Thus, "the All-conquering" (القاهر) is among the names that are true of Him, the Exalted, as they are true of others, except that between His conquest, the Exalted, and the conquest of others, there is a difference. The difference is that others besides Him, the Exalted, among things, only conquer one another while they are joined in the rank of their existence and the degree of their being—meaning that fire conquers wood into burning and igniting, and both together are natural existents, one of which naturally requires the opposite of what the other requires, but the fire is stronger in imposing its effect upon the wood than the wood is upon the fire, so it prevails over it in its influence with its effect upon it.

But Allah, the Glorified, is All-conquering, not like the conquest of fire over wood; rather, He is All-conquering by superiority (التفوق) and absolute encompassment (الإحاطة على الإطلاق). This means that if we attribute the burning and igniting of a body like wood, for example, to Allah, the Glorified, then He, the Glorified, is All-conquering over it by the limited existence with which He brought it into being; All-conquering over it by the properties and qualities He gave it and equipped it with by His hand; All-conquering over it by the fire He kindled for its burning and igniting—and He is the Owner of all that the fire possesses of essence and effect; All-conquering over it by cutting off the gift of resistance from the wood and placing burning and igniting in its stead. Thus, there is no resistance, no disobedience, no rebellion, nor anything similar before His will and His desire, because they are from a higher horizon.

So He, the Exalted, is All-conquering over His servants, but He is "above them" (فَوْقَهُمْ), not like the conquest of one thing over another while they are peers. The Quran has confirmed this discussion with its conclusion, mentioning it as a name for Him, the Exalted, in two places in this Surah: this verse and verse 61.

He restricted the name in both places with His saying: **فَوْقَ عِبادِهِ** (above His servants). The majority of preserved instances of the use of "conquest" (القهر) is that the overcome be among the possessors of intellect (أولي العقل), unlike "overcoming" (الغلبة). For this reason, al-Raghib explained it as "subjugation" (التذليل), and submissiveness (الذلة) is more apparent in the possessors of intellect. This does not prevent the correctness of its application in cases other than the possessors of intellect according to usage or by figurative extension.

Allah, the Glorified, is the All-conquering above His servants; He visits them with distress and with good and subjugates them to His compliance. He is the All-conquering above His servants in what they do and the effects they produce, because He is the Owner of what He made them own and the One powerful over what He empowered them with.

Since in the two verses He attributed to Himself the visiting with distress and good, and they might be attributed to others, He distinguished His station from the station of others by His saying at the end of the verse: **وَهُوَ الْحَكِيمُ الْخَبِيرُ** (and He is the All-wise, the All-aware). So He is the All-wise (الحكيم) who does not do what He does haphazardly or out of ignorance, and the All-aware (الخبير) who does not err or make mistakes like others.

***

(1) The elite among the pagans, although they might permit His worship based on the fact that He is of unlimited existence and worshipful focus cannot attach to Him, the commoners among them perhaps worshipped Him alongside other deities, as appears from the *talbiyah* of the polytheists of Mecca during Hajj: "At Your service, You have no partner, except a partner who is Yours; You own him and what he owns."
